coredata.dc:title: Gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol)-based functional films integrated with spent coffee ground-derived carbon dots and grapefruit seed extract for active packaging application
coredata.dc:creator: Min, Seungjae
Ezati, Parya
Yoon, Ki Sun
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Nanoparticles are attractive, functional additives with great potential to be applied in biomaterial and food packaging. However, these particles are not soluble in water, thus limiting their widespread application. Here, we report a facile fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) using the spent coffee ground as the carbon source through a hydrothermal method. The CD was added to the gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol) (Gel/PVA) film and grapefruit seed extract (GSE) to prepare multifunctional packaging films. The functional films' physiochemical and functional properties and packaging application were investigated. The composite film showed good UV protection properties with a slight decrease in transparency. The composite film containing CD/GSE showed strong antioxidant activity, scavenging >38 % DPPH and 100 % ABTS radicals. The film also exhibited significant antibacterial activity against the foodborne pathogens Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli, completely eradicating the growth of these bacteria within 9 h of exposure. The CD/GSE-incorporated Gel/PVA films were used for pork packaging. The films were able to enhance the pork shelf life by reducing the L. monocytogenes bacterial growth in meat by 2 Log CFU/mL lower than the control wrapping film. The multifunctional Gel/PVA films are expected to be used for the active packaging of meat products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spent coffee ground
Carbon dots
Grapefruit seed extract
Antimicrobial
Antioxidant
Active packaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Carbohydrate polymers: Production technologies, industrial and environmental applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity (MTT assay) of the CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean Government ( MSIT ) (No. 2022R1A2B5B02001422 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of the CDs with different magnifications and the visible photoluminescence of the CD solution under UV light, (b) FTIR, and (c) XRD spectra of the CDs.
Effect of carbon dots concentration on the viability of mouse fibroblast L929 cells after 48 h treatment. Identical letters above the bar indicate no significant difference (p > 0.05).
Apparent (a) and SEM images showing the surface morphologies (b) and cross-sectional (c) views of Gel/PVA, Gel/PVA/GSE, Gel/PVA/CD, and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD films.
FTIR patterns of Gel/PVA, Gel/PVA/GSE, Gel/PVA/CD, and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD films.

Antioxidant activity of CDs (a) and developed film including Gel/PVA, Gel/PVA/GSE, Gel/PVA/CD, and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD films (b). Different letters for each DPPH and ABTS method indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).
Antibacterial activity of Gel/PVA, Gel/PVA/GSE, Gel/PVA/CD, and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD films.
Changes in the appearance of minced pork wrapped with LDPE and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film irradiated with UV light at 20 °C for 2 days.
Effect of packaging on (a) bleaching of meat and (b) the growth of Listeria monocytogenes in pork wrapped with LDPE and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD films at 20 °C for 2 days.
The schematic diagram for the fabrication of Gel/PVA-based films.
Surface color and light transmittance of Gel/PVA, Gel/PVA/GSE, Gel/PVA/CD, and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as a mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanical properties, WVP, and WCA of Gel/PVA, Gel/PVA/GSE, Gel/PVA/CD, and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as a mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol)-based functional films integrated with spent coffee ground-derived carbon dots and grapefruit seed extract for active packaging application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoparticles are attractive, functional additives with great potential to be applied in biomaterial and food packaging. However, these particles are not soluble in water, thus limiting their widespread application. Here, we report a facile fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) using the spent coffee ground as the carbon source through a hydrothermal method. The CD was added to the gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol) (Gel/PVA) film and grapefruit seed extract (GSE) to prepare multifunctional packaging films. The functional films' physiochemical and functional properties and packaging application were investigated. The composite film showed good UV protection properties with a slight decrease in transparency. The composite film containing CD/GSE showed strong antioxidant activity, scavenging >38 % DPPH and 100 % ABTS radicals. The film also exhibited significant antibacterial activity against the foodborne pathogens Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli, completely eradicating the growth of these bacteria within 9 h of exposure. The CD/GSE-incorporated Gel/PVA films were used for pork packaging. The films were able to enhance the pork shelf life by reducing the L. monocytogenes bacterial growth in meat by 2 Log CFU/mL lower than the control wrapping film. The multifunctional Gel/PVA films are expected to be used for the active packaging of meat products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Coffee is one of the most consumed beverages in the world, with approximately 1 billion kg of coffee consumed worldwide each year. The most common way to make a coffee drink from coffee beans is to grind roasted coffee beans to make an espresso, which is brewed hydraulically [1]. As a result of this extraction process, waste coffee grounds are generated, and as coffee consumption increases, the production of waste coffee grounds, a by-product, is also increasing. In general, the generated coffee grounds are discarded, but as the importance of sustainable synthesis has recently been highlighted, research to recycle spent coffee grounds is being actively conducted, including biodiesel production, antioxidants, subgrade filler material, etc. [2]. Spent coffee grounds are made up of minerals, polyphenols, lipids, and carbohydrates, so they can be used to make a variety of synthetic substances [3].

One possible application of spent coffee grounds is synthesizing sustainable carbon dots. Carbon dot (CD) is a carbon-based photoluminescent nanomaterial having a size of <10 nm [4,5]. CD can be easily synthesized using various sustainable biomass raw materials such as agricultural waste and microorganisms [6,7]. The carbohydrate content of the spent coffee grounds ranges from 63 to 83 %, making it a good material for potential high-yield CD synthesis due to its high carbon content [8]. CD has been utilized for human contact biological applications such as cell imaging due to its desirable properties, such as hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, and safety [9,10]. In addition, the CD has various functional properties such as UV-barrier, antibacterial, antioxidant, and antifungal properties, making it suitable for practical uses such as food packaging and preservation, and is used as a multifunctional filler for biopolymer-based packaging films [11,12].

Biopolymer-based functional films have attracted the attention of researchers over the past few decades. This film has the potential to replace petroleum-based, non-biodegradable plastics that pose serious environmental hazards [13]. In addition, biopolymer-based films have additional properties not found in synthetic polymers, such as biocompatibility, the ability to transport and release functional ingredients, and in some cases, antioxidant and antibacterial properties [14,15]. However, a biopolymer film prepared using a single biopolymer would be inefficient in maintaining all the properties required for packaging applications. Therefore, blend films developed by tailoring two or more biopolymers have attracted attention. For example, a blend film of gelatin (Gel) and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) has been developed to tailor the properties of Gel (biocompatibility, biodegradability, and bioactivity) and PVA (optical transparency, mechanical strength, biocompatibility, and thermal stability) [16,17].

Gelatin is a biopolymer derived from animal proteins, which is typically created by denaturing and hydrolyzing collagen [18]. Gelatin is utilized extensively in the food industry because of its outstanding gelling and thickening properties. Due to their excellent gas barrier and mechanical properties, Gel-based films are often used for food packaging applications [14,19]. Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) is an eco-friendly polymer prepared by hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate, has a skeleton containing only carbon atoms, and is biodegradable under aerobic or anaerobic conditions [20,21]. As a binder and coating agent, PVA has a variety of uses in the food industry in applications that require moisture barrier and water-resistant properties [22]. Due to its excellent film-forming property, complete biodegradability, and high crystallinity, PVA has been used for packaging film manufacturing [23].

Biopolymer-based active packaging films are manufactured with various functional fillers to improve physicochemical quality and impart functional properties such as antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV protection [7]. Among various functional materials, in particular, nanomaterials such as CD attract attention because they have a reinforcing effect that improves mechanical and functional properties [12]. Also, a natural plant extract, grapefruit seed extract (GSE), attracts attention in food packaging for its excellent antibacterial properties [24,25]. GSE contains flavonoid components such as Naringin and phytochemicals such as limonoids, showing an antioxidant effect [26].

Gel/PVA-based films were characterized and studied for their physicochemical and functional properties, and their potential in food packaging applications was evaluated through ground pork packaging experiments. Therefore, in this study, the CD was synthesized by hydrothermal method using spent coffee ground and used as a nanofiller for Gel/PVA blend film. Grapefruit seed extract (GSE) was also added to the film to increase the film's functional properties synergistically. Based on these characteristics of CD and GSE, this study aims to develop sustainable packaging materials for active packaging applications based on Gel/PVA and GSE/CD ().

Gel/PVA-based films were prepared using a solution casting process, as shown in Scheme 1 . Gel/PVA film solution was prepared by dissolving 3 g of Gel and 1 g of PVA in 150 mL of distilled water with 1.2 g of glycerol (30 wt% based on polymer) as a plasticizer while stirring vigorously for 30 min at 90 °C. In addition, three different functional film solutions of Gel/PVA/CD, Gel/PVA/GSE, and Gel/PVA/GSE/CD were prepared by dissolving 0.12 g CD, 0.12 g GSE, and 0.12 g CD + 0.12 g GSE, respectively, to the Gel/PVA film solution following the same procedure. The characterization and the determination of the composite films were explained in Supporting Information.

Ground pork (10 g) was packed in a bag (7 cm × 7 cm) made of the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD composite film. In addition, a commercially used plastic bag made of low-density polyethylene (LDPE) was used for comparison. The packaged samples were stored in a sterile air hood under UV lighting for 48 h. The redness of the meat was evaluated by determining Hunter a-value using a colorimeter (Konica Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) at predetermined time intervals.

The efficacy of the packaging film for preventing the growth of foodborne pathogenic bacteria, ground pork was inoculated with L. monocytogenes and packed with the test film. For this, 10 g of ground pork was inoculated with 200 μL of L. monocytogenes culture (~3 Log CFU/mL) and dried for 5 min in a sterile air hood. The inoculated meat was wrapped in a Gel/PVA/GSE/CD composite film and an LDPE film pouch and stored in a low-temperature incubator at 20 °C for 48 h. A packaged sample from each group was unwrapped on 0, 12, 24, and 48 h, and the microbial count on the meat was estimated using a total colony count method as described by Priyadarshi et al. [27].

TEM images of the CD are shown in Fig. 1a, which shows an approximately spherical morphology of the CD with an average particle size of 8.37 nm and good dispersion without aggregation, which is in good agreement with that previously reported [7,28].

FTIR spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 1b. The absorption band at 3242 cm−1 is attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations, while those around 2926 cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching vibrations in the CD structure. Also, a sharp absorption band was observed at 1769 cm−1, which is attributed to the carbonyl (C=O) stretching, indicating the presence of carboxylate and hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of the CDs [29]. The absorption bands corresponding to NH bending vibrations at 1660 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 and CH and CN bending vibrations between 1000 and 1200 cm−1 were also observed [30]. These bands indicate the presence of nitrogenous functional groups in the CDs, which may have originated from the nitrogenous polyphenolic compounds in coffee. Further, the bands at 1386 cm−1 and 677 cm−1 were observed due to the CC and CH (alkyne) vibration in the CD structure [31]. The XRD spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 1c. Two peaks were found in the XRD spectrum of the CDs and were found at 2θ = 23.2° and 40.6°. The detected peaks indicate (002) and (100) diffraction of graphite carbon [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity (MTT assay) of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was performed to determine the in vitro cytotoxicity of CD at doses ranging from 0.1 to 1 mg/mL against the L929 mouse fibroblast line, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . >80 % cell viability was observed at all tested concentrations compared to control samples, which could be considered safe for biological applications [33]. The results indicate lower toxicity and better biocompatibility of CD. These findings showed that CD, a sustainable, biocompatible nanomaterial with low cytotoxicity, is ideal for food packaging applications.

Apparent images and SEM micrographs, including the surface morphology and cross-sectional microstructures of the fabricated Gel/PVA and GSE and CDs, incorporated Gel/PVA-based composite films are presented in Fig. 3 . After the development of composite films, images of the Gel/PVA-based films with GSE and CDs incorporation were obtained and are displayed in Fig. 3a. The addition of GSE did not affect the appearance of the film and made the film slightly translucent, but the addition of CD gives the film a light brown color due to the tan of the CD.

SEM images (Fig. 3b) show that the neat Gel/PVA film has a smooth, continuous, and dense surface with no impurities or cracks and voids in the film, indicating Gel is compatibly blended with PVA. Similar compatible blend structures were observed in the Gel with other biopolymers such as chitosan, pectin, PVA, etc. [11,17,34,35]. The absence of surface cavities or cracks in the Gel/PVA/GSE film indicates that the GSE is compatible with the polymer matrix as the surface morphology is not affected (Fig. 3b). This may be due to the strong interaction of GSE with Gel and PVA and their hydrophilic structure. In contrast, some small lumps were observed, and the surface of the Gel/PVA/CD film appeared to be significantly rougher than the neat Gel/PVA film. However, the CD was evenly distributed throughout the polymer matrix. Although some small lumps were observed after adding the CDs (Gel/PVA/CD film), they were uniformly dispersed throughout the polymer matrix, indicating that the added components were compatible with the polymer matrix. The granular mass increased in the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film, and the surface looks much wavier than the neat Gel/PVA film. The granular surface appearance is most likely due to increased CD accumulation in the Gel/PVA blend. Cross-sectional images also revealed the continuous internal structure and the homogeneous dispersion of the filler in the polymer matrix, showing good compatibility with the fillers (GSE and CD) and Gel/PVA polymer matrix (Fig. 3c).

The functional groups and structural characteristics of the Gel/PVA-based films were evaluated using the FTIR test, and their FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 4 . The transmittance peaks positioned at 3280 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 can be ascribed due to OH and CH stretching of the Gel and PVA chain [36]. The characteristic peak around 1446 and 1236 cm−1 was attributed to the Gel's stretching vibration of CN and NH [18]. In the case of the PVA film, the peaks around 1740 cm−1 assign CO stretching; the peaks at about 1446 cm−1 were attributed due to the stretching vibration modes of -CH2; and the absorption at 1084 cm−1 was attributed to C-O-C stretching [37,38]. As demonstrated in Fig. 4, for the Gel/PVA films with GSE and CD, the characteristic peak overlapped with the similar -OH, CH, CO, CN, and NH stretching vibration of the Gel/PVA polymer blend in the same region. The FTIR spectra show that the peaks' intensities reduced without changing the frequency regions in the spectra of the films incorporated with GSE and CD, indicating that the GSE and CD were thoroughly blended with the Gel/PVA polymer matrix to make a compatible film.

Packaging films' transparency and optical properties are key factors in securing visibility and satisfying customers. It is well known that adding active nanofillers to biopolymer-based active films changes their appearance by imparting appropriate functional properties [39]. Therefore, it is important to check the color and optical properties of the packaging film. Table 1 shows the color properties and light transmittance of the Gel/PVA-based films. The lightness (L-value) of the Gel/PVA film did not change significantly (p < 0.05) with the addition of GSE but significantly decreased (p > 0.05) with the addition of CD. The greenness (a-value) and yellowness (b-value) increased significantly with the addition of GSE; however, they increased further with CD. Consequently, the total color difference (ΔE) increased slightly with the addition of GSE but increased profoundly with the CD. The slightly increased yellowness of the film by the addition of GSE was due to the pale-yellow color of GSE, and the increased yellowness of the CD-added films was attributed to the brown color of the CD (Fig. 3a).

The transparency and UV-barrier properties of the film evaluated by measuring their light transmittance at 660 and 280 nm, respectively, are shown in Table 1. The neat Gel/PVA is very transparent with 87.6 % T660 and has relatively high UV-blocking properties with 16.4 % T280. The low T280 of the neat Gel/PVA film is due to the UV absorption by the aromatic amino acids of Gel molecules [18,40]. In contrast, PVA films are very transparent to UV light, and neat PVA films have been reported to have 51 % T280 [41]. The addition of GSE reduced the T280 significantly, but the T660 was not significantly changed. The increased UV barrier property of the GSE-added film was mainly due to the polyphenol component of GSE absorbing UV light [24]. Similar increases in UV blocking properties have been observed with the addition of GSE to other biopolymer-based films, such as pectin/agar and carrageenan films [42].

In contrast, the addition of CD reduced the T280 down to 0.2 %, with a slight decrease in the T660, indicating the CD-added film blocks the UV light entirely with a slight sacrifice to the transparency of the film. Various factors affect the UV-blocking properties of CDs, such as the size of the π-community domain, the type and composition of surface groups, and the oxygen/nitrogen ratio of the carbon core [7,11,43]. Intramolecular proton transport through O-H-N, O-H-O tunnels, and appropriate junction structures provide the UV barrier properties of CD-added films. When CD and GSE were added simultaneously, the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film almost completely blocked UV transmittance at 0.1 % of T280 but maintained high transparency with T660 of 86.5 %. Transparent and excellent UV protection films such as the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film are expected to be used as UV protection packaging materials that protect food from photochemical reactions caused by UV rays in the food packaging field [44].

The thickness, mechanical, water vapor permeability (WVP), and water contact angle (WCA) properties of the Gel/PVA-based composite films are shown in Table 2 . There was no noticeable difference in thickness when GSE was added to the neat Gel/PVA film, which had a thickness of 62.2 ± 2.1 μm. However, the thickness increased to 67.9 ± 2.6 and 71.1 ± 1.5 μm when CD and CD/GSE were included. The neat Gel/PVA film was relatively strong and stiff, with low flexibility with tensile strength (TS) of 75.2 MPa, elongation at break (EB) of 13.5 %, and modulus of elasticity (EM) of 2.8 GPa. The addition of GSE slightly decreased the TS of the film but significantly decreased with the addition of CD, indicating that CD has a weaker interaction with the polymer matrix than GSE. Similar to the results of this study, it was reported that the mechanical properties were not changed or damaged even when GSE was added to the carrageenan-based film [45,46], and the TS of the Gel-based film was significantly reduced when a high concentration of CD was added [47]. The EB of the film was significantly decreased by the addition of GSE and CD, whereas the EM of the Gel/PVA film was not significantly different.

The WVP of the neat Gel/PVA film was 6.2 ± 0.1 × 10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s, which is in the range of Gel and PVA films [42]. The WVP of the film increased significantly with the addition of GSE and more significantly decreased with the addition of CD, probably due to the hydrophilic properties of the GSE and CD. Adding GSE and CD increases the number of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups in the film, increasing the interaction with water molecules and thereby increasing WVP. Previous studies have reported similar results of increasing WVP by adding GSE and CD to carboxymethyl cellulose and gelatin-based biopolymer films [48,49].

Table 2 shows the WCA of the Gel/PVA-based films. Generally, in most biopolymer films, the film's surface becomes hydrophilic due to the hydrophilic groups such as -OH and -NH2 groups of the film matrix [4]. The WCA of the neat Gel/PVA film was 66.5°, indicating the Gel/PVA film has a slightly hydrophobic surface. However, the addition of GSE and CD significantly reduced the hydrophobicity of the film, which is due to the hydrophilicity of the fillers. Similar to the present results, adding GSE and CD decreased the WCA of biopolymer-based films such as cellulose nanofibers, carboxymethyl cellulose, and pectin/agar films [30,42,50].

Fig. 5a shows the antioxidant activity of the spent coffee ground-derived CD determined by the ABTS and DPPH methods. The CD showed significant antioxidant activity, and even at 50 μg/mL of CD, it showed 71.5 ± 3 % and 32.5 ± 0.9 % activity against ABTS and DPPH methods. The antioxidant activities increased with the CD concentration. The CD showed higher antioxidant activity in the ABTS method than in the DPPH method because the hydrophilic CD was more readily eluted from the aqueous solution of ABTS than from the methanol solution of DPPH. The ABTS• and DPPH• scavenging effect is because the -OH of the CD is transferred to ABTS-H and DPPH-H [51,52].

The antioxidant activity of the Gel/PVA-based films determined by the ABTS and DPPH methods are shown in Fig. 5b. The neat Gel/PVA film showed some antioxidant activity of 5.8 ± 0.7 % and 35.6 ± 1.6 % for the DPPH and ABTS methods. The slight antioxidant activity of the neat Gel/PVA film may be attributed to the free amino groups of the Gel molecule having free radical scavenging activity [53]. Adding GSE and CD increased the antioxidant activity of the films significantly. The surface hydroxyl groups of GSE and CD-added biopolymer films may be responsible for their antioxidant action [54]. However, the antioxidant activity of the film was more significantly increased when CD was added than when GSE was added, indicating that CD has potent antioxidant activity. The antioxidant activity of GSE is attributed to the various flavonoids found in GSE [24]. On the other hand, the CD can neutralize free radicals and exert antioxidant activity by giving and receiving electrons that interact with free radicals through an electron transport mechanism [29]. Recently, antioxidant films have been developed by adding CD to other carbohydrate polymers such as cellulose nanofibers and furcellaran [30,55] and by adding GSE to PVA-based films [38]. This study is the first to investigate the synergistic effect of antioxidant activities of CD and GSE. As a result, the GSE and CD-added Gel/PVA film showed excellent antioxidant activity. These antioxidant packaging materials are expected to prolong shelf life by preventing the oxidative degradation of packaged foods.

The antibacterial activity of the Gel/PVA-based films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes bacteria is shown in Fig. 6 . The neat Gel/PVA film showed no antibacterial activity against both tested bacteria showing bacterial growth similar to the control. When CD was added to the Gel/PVA film, the growth rate was slightly reduced compared to the neat Gel/PVA film, but no significant reduction in bacterial growth was observed. It has been reported that CD showed some antibacterial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogenes [47]. In contrast, adding GSE to the film significantly increased antibacterial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogenes. Namely, the GSE-added films stopped the bacterial growth of E. coli and L. monocytogenes entirely after 9 h exposure. GSE is known to have strong antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria because it can easily penetrate the cell wall of bacteria, interfere with bacterial metabolic processes, and inhibit growth [37,56]. Such a strong antibacterial effect was observed in a film based on carboxymethyl cellulose, to which GSE was added [57]. Polyphenolic compounds such as naringin and limonin play essential roles in the antibacterial activity of GSE [26]. Naringin and limonin are thought to inhibit bacterial growth by penetrating bacterial cell membranes, binding to cellular proteins, and inactivating their functions [24]. Composite films containing GSE and CD also showed strong antibacterial activity, mainly attributed to GSE.

Ultraviolet radiation (below 400 nm) seriously affects food's flavor, color, nutritional content, and bioactivity. Early signs of UV-mediated food degradation are changes in appearance, including discoloration. The UV blocking efficacy of the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film to prevent discoloration of minced pork stored under UV lighting was tested compared to LDPE film. Fig. 7 shows the apparent change of the minced pork during storage under UV light at 20 °C. In general, the surface color of packaged meat was darkened by bleaching of the red color of the meat over time during storage under UV light. The discoloration of meat during storage is due to converting myoglobin, the red pigment in raw meat, to brown met-myoglobin by UV-catalyzed photo-oxidation [58]. In LDPE packaged meat, bleaching was more severe than in the GEL/PVA/GSE/CD film packaged meat, and it turned dark brown within one day of storage. Meat wrapped in LDPE film had a rancid odor after 24 h storage under UV light, whereas meat wrapped in Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film showed no odor or other negative features with little color change. The change in the a-value representing the redness of the meat was determined using a colorimeter, and the results are shown in Fig. 8a. The a-value of meat wrapped with the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film was significantly lower than that of meat wrapped with LDPE film, indicating the UV protection effect of the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film. Therefore, the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film with excellent UV protection function is expected to effectively block UV rays and prevent photo-oxidation of food and maintain quality and extend shelf life.

The antibacterial efficacy of the film was evaluated through the packaging of minced pork inoculated with L. monocytogenes, and the results are shown in Fig. 8b. L. monocytogenes in LDPE film packaged meat increased rapidly from 3 Log CFU/mL to 7 Log CFU/mL after 12 h of incubation and showed the same level of cells after that. In contrast, L. monocytogenes in meat packaged with the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film increased to 4 Log CFU/mL at 12 h incubation and maintained the same level of cells after that. The Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film reduced L. monocytogenes proliferation by 3 Log CFU/mL more than the control LDPE film, indicating the antibacterial efficiency of the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film. The Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film did not completely reduce L. monocytogenes in packaged meat but showed a bacteriostatic effect in preventing the further growth of bacteria. The Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film's antibacterial activity mainly comes from GSE (Fig. 6). Overall, Gel/PVA/GSE/CD film provides excellent UV protection and can be utilized to block UV rays in food packaging to prevent photooxidation effectively.

Gelatin/PVA-based multifunctional films were prepared using a solvent casting method by adding blue-luminescent CD prepared using spent coffee grounds and grapefruit seed extract (GSE) for active food packaging applications. FT-IR and SEM profiles showed strong compatibility and intermolecular H bonds between CD/GSE and the polymer matrix. In particular, the UV blocking ability of the fabricated films was greatly improved with the addition of CD and GSE, but the mechanical properties of the composite films decreased. The composite films with CD/GSE ensured pork safety and improved shelf life during the 48-h storage period at 20 °C. During the storage period, minced pork samples showed the lowest bacterial counts (<4 Log CFU/g after 24 h) with minimal change in the a-values (least discoloration). Therefore, the Gel/PVA/GSE/CD composite film is expected to be used in active packaging applications that prevent quality deterioration of meat products due to photooxidation and prevent microbial deterioration to ensure food safety.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean Government (MSIT) (No. 2022R1A2B5B02001422).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.123493.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of different coffee storage methods on coffee freshness after opening of packages
Food Packag. Shelf Life

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [1] S. Smrke, J. Adam, S. Mühlemann, I. Lantz, C. Yeretzian, Effects of different coffee storage methods on coffee freshness after opening of packages, Food Packag. Shelf Life. 33 (2022) 100893. doi:10.1016/j.fpsl.2022.100893.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Valorization of spent coffee grounds: a sustainable resource for bio-based phase change materials for thermal energy storage
Waste Manag.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [2] P. Jin Ong, Y. Leow, X. Yun Debbie Soo, M. Hui Chua, X. Ni, A. Suwardi, C. Kiang Ivan Tan, R. Zheng, F. Wei, J. Xu, X. Jun Loh, D. Kai, Q. Zhu, Valorization of spent coffee grounds: A sustainable resource for bio-based phase change materials for thermal energy storage, Waste Manag. 157 (2023) 339–347. doi:10.1016/j.wasman.2022.12.039.
